key_insight: A loss-of-function IL-34 variant appears to lower IL-34 levels and push microglia toward Alzheimer’s disease-associated, inflammatory states linked to amyloid pathology.
clinical_relevance: These findings suggest IL-34 may be a human genetic modifier of neuroinflammation and microglial health in Alzheimer’s disease, making it a potential biomarker or target for future study.
method: Measured IL-34 in CSF and serum from deeply phenotyped AD cohorts stratified by IL-34-Y213X genotype.
Integrated IL-34 status with CSF proteomics, transcriptomics, and phenome-wide association analyses.
Tested IL-34 deficiency in mouse microglia, APP/PS1 mice, and postmortem human AD cortex.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports multi-cohort human genetics plus biomarker, proteomic, transcriptomic, mouse, and postmortem tissue data showing consistent effects of IL-34 deficiency on microglial and AD-related signatures.
watch_item: Associations and mouse-model effects are strong but do not by themselves prove that restoring IL-34 will safely improve human Alzheimer’s outcomes.
paper_title: Human IL-34 Deficiency Primes Microglia Toward Alzheimer's Disease-Associated States
